summary: Gruber dissects Intel's new ad campaign starring Justin Long — the actor who once personified 'I'm a Mac' — now pitching for PCs. He finds the campaign cringey and conceptually hollow, noting the irony that Long was always indirectly pitching Intel-based computers during the original Apple ads. Gruber argues the campaign highlights the unusual nature of Apple's 15-year Intel relationship, where neither company ever advertised their partnership. He contends that Intel's strongest argument — PC gaming superiority — actually undermines Intel's own position, since the Mac's gaming weakness during the Intel era suggests the problem was never chip architecture. The campaign feels entirely defensive, aimed at preventing Mac users from switching rather than winning new converts.
key_points: The campaign's only concept is hiring Justin Long, which isn't clever enough to sustain multiple spots — it's neither parody nor sequel to the original Get a Mac ads
Apple and Intel had a uniquely non-promotional 15-year relationship: Apple never advertised Macs as Intel-based, and Intel never mentioned Macs in commercials
Intel bragging about PC gaming superiority is self-defeating — the Mac's weak gaming position during 15 years on Intel chips suggests the issue is Windows vs. macOS, not chip architecture
Intel is losing the CPU race to AMD in the PC gaming space, making their gaming argument even weaker
The campaign is fundamentally defensive ('don't switch') compared to Apple's original offensive strategy ('try a Mac')
money_quotes: It's an attempt at comedy from writers who have no sense of humor.
One gets the feeling, early on, that there was an uncomfortable phone call to Justin Long from his agent that began, 'Before you say no, at least let me tell you how much money they're offering.'
Apple's not moving the Mac from Intel to its own chips because it was the easier path.

It's almost enough to make you think that being on Intel didn't do much for the Mac, gaming-wise, and that whatever the reasons for the Mac's position as a relative afterthought in gaming, it has a lot more to do with MacOS vs. Windows than CPU chip architecture.
We talk about it in the past tense but it's like a divorce where one of the spouses still hasn't moved most of their stuff out of the apartment.
The point of these ads feels entirely defensive, reactionary even: Don't switch.
Apple could have stayed on Intel for as long as they wanted, and instead chose to spend the last 10 years building up their own chip designs so they could leave Intel behind.
key_insight: Intel's campaign inadvertently highlights that after 15 years of Intel-based Macs, Apple chose to leave — and that Intel's strongest arguments against the Mac actually indict Intel's own platform more than Apple's.
